items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Earthquakes in Venezuela: Situation Report #10 (03 July 2026, Time: 07:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Situation Report No. 10
Earthquakes in Venezuela
03 July 2026
Time: 7:00 pm

Highlights

  • Following the 24 June earthquakes of magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 (18:00 local time), authorities report more than 800 aftershocks.
  • At least seven (7) states have been impacted. La Guaira remains the most affected state.
  • Official toll: 2,645 people dead and 12,666 injured.
  • A total of 6,462 people have been rescued.
  • Urban Search and Rescue (USAR) teams remain deployed in the affected areas, in coordination with OCHA, more than one week after the earthquakes.
  • The United Nations system and its partner organizations, in coordination with the Government, are responding with multisectoral actions and will continue to scale up assistance to affected people.

Situation overview

Venezuela is facing a national emergency following two consecutive high-magnitude earthquakes (7.2 and 7.5) that struck on 24 June (18:00 local time) in the north-central region, with the epicentre located along the San Felipe–Yumare–Montalbán axis (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Authorities report 890 aftershocks.
More than a week after the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 2,645 people dead, 12,666 injured, and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency. Authorities also reported that approximately 15,050 people lost their homes.
The Government response remains focused on search and rescue operations, the preparation and expansion of transitional camps and and the assistance to affected populations. La Guaira remains the most affected state. The United Nations and its partner organizations, in coordination with the authorities, are implementing multisectoral response activities and will continue to scale up assistance to affected people.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Terremotos en Venezuela: Reporte de situación #10 (03 de julio de 2026, Hora: 07:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Reporte de situación N.º 10
Terremotos en Venezuela
3 de julio de 2026
Hora: 7:00 pm

Destacados

  • Tras los sismos de magnitud 7,2 y 7,5 del 24 de junio (18:00 hora local), las autoridades reportan 890 réplicas.
  • Al menos siete (7) estados impactados. La Guaira se mantiene como el estado más afectado.
  • Balance oficial: 2.645 personas fallecidas y 12.666 heridas.
  • 6.462 personas han sido rescatadas.
  • Los equipos de Búsqueda y Rescate Urbano (USAR) continúan desplegados en las zonas afectadas, en coordinación con OCHA, a más de una semana de los terremotos.
  • El Sistema de Naciones Unidas y sus organizaciones socias, en coordinación con el gobierno, responden con acciones multisectoriales y continuarán ampliando la asistencia a las personas afectadas.

Situación general

Venezuela enfrenta una emergencia nacional tras dos sismos consecutivos de gran magnitud (7,2 y 7,5) ocurridos el 24 de junio (18:00, hora local) en la región centro-norte, con epicentro sobre el eje San Felipe - Yumare - Montalbán (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Las autoridades reportan 890 réplicas.

A más de una semana de los terremotos, las autoridades reportan un balance de 2.645 personas fallecidas, 12.666 personas heridas y 6.462 personas rescatadas desde el inicio de la emergencia. Asimismo, las autoridades reportan que unas 15.050 personas perdieron sus viviendas.
La respuesta gubernamental mantiene el foco en las labores de búsqueda, la preparación de los campamentos transitorios y la atención de las personas afectadas. La Guaira se mantiene como el estado más afectado. Naciones Unidas y sus organizaciones socias, en articulación con las autoridades, realizan acciones multisectoriales para las personas afectadas y continuarán ampliando la respuesta.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of): Earthquake Response Situation Report #5 (2 July 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

The fifth Situation Report provides an update on the impact of the 24 June earthquakes in the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela and summarizes IOM’s ongoing and planned response in support of affected populations and national efforts.

Highlights

As of 2 July 2026, official figures reported at least 2,595 fatalities and 12,400 injuries following the 24 June earthquakes. Figures pending further update include 12,841 severely affected people, 26,403 affected people, 855 affected buildings, 189 total collapses and 6,461 people rescued.

IOM continues supporting temporary sites, multidisciplinary health and MHPSS services, protection, community engagement, registration, information management and needs assessments in La Guaira and Falcón. Urgent additional support is required to scale up life-saving assistance, improve conditions in temporary sites, strengthen referral, protection and accountability mechanisms, and generate timely data to inform a safe, dignified and targeted response.

IOM activities

IOM has continued supporting shelter, site coordination and essential services in temporary sites in La Guaira, including the Polideportivo José María Vargas and the Estadio César Nieves, where 1,200 and 1,500 people have been registered, respectively. At the Polideportivo José María Vargas, IOM supported the installation of 150 bunk beds, received tarpaulins and other supplies, strengthened site organization and held two community assemblies with leaders to define roles and responsibilities for site maintenance.

In health and MHPSS, IOM-supported teams assisted 297 people at the Polideportivo José María Vargas, 185 people at the Estadio César Nieves and 97 people in Cayumar, Falcón, through general medicine, paediatrics, internal medicine, gynaecology, psychological first aid and psychosocial support services. IOM also mobilized a water treatment plant, continued providing protection orientation and referrals, provided 13 legal counselling sessions, identified the presence of unaccompanied children and adolescents in Falcón, trained enumerators on the IMS registration tool and, together with OCHA, completed three Rapid Needs Assessment surveys in Silva municipality.

IOM planned response

IOM will continue prioritizing safe temporary accommodation, site coordination and management, WASH, health and MHPSS, protection, data and needs assessments. In temporary sites, IOM will continue supporting site planning, registration, warehouse management, tent installation, lighting, charging points, connectivity, community engagement, information provision, AAP, GBV risk mitigation and PSEA measures.

In WASH, health, protection and information management, IOM will seek to expand access to safe water, basic sanitation, hygiene promotion, mobile health services, referrals, care for chronic conditions, sexual and reproductive health, psychological first aid and MHPSS. This document also highlights needs for additional support to sustain and scale up humanitarian assistance, improve conditions in temporary sites and affected communities, strengthen safe and accountable services, reduce protection and public health risks, and generate timely data to inform a more targeted response for affected populations.

Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Highlights

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Venezuela (República Bolivariana de): Respuesta a Terremotos - Reporte de Situación #5 (2 de julio de 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

El quinto Informe de Situación presenta una actualización, sobre el impacto de los terremotos del 24 de junio en la República Bolivariana de Venezuela y resume la respuesta en curso y planificada de la OIM en apoyo a las poblaciones afectadas y a los esfuerzos nacionales.

Aspectos destacados

Al 2 de julio de 2026, las cifras oficiales reportaron al menos 2.595 personas fallecidas y 12.400 personas heridas tras los terremotos del 24 de junio. Las cifras pendientes de nueva actualización incluyen 12.841 personas damnificadas, 26.403 personas afectadas, 855 edificaciones afectadas, 189 colapsos totales y 6.461 personas rescatadas.

La OIM continúa apoyando campamentos transitorios, servicios multidisciplinarios de salud y SMAPS, protección, participación comunitaria, registro, gestión de información y evaluaciones de necesidades en La Guaira y Falcón. Se requiere apoyo adicional urgente para ampliar la asistencia vital, mejorar las condiciones en campamentos transitorios, fortalecer mecanismos de derivación, protección y rendición de cuentas, y generar datos oportunos para orientar una respuesta segura, digna y focalizada.

Actividades de la OIM

La OIM ha continuado apoyando alojamiento, coordinación de sitios y servicios esenciales en campamentos transitorios en La Guaira, incluidos el Polideportivo José María Vargas y el Estadio César Nieves, donde se han registrado 1.200 y 1.500 personas, respectivamente. En el Polideportivo José María Vargas, la OIM apoyó la instalación de 150 literas, recibió tarpaulins y otros insumos, fortaleció la organización del espacio y realizó dos asambleas comunitarias con líderes para definir roles y responsabilidades en el mantenimiento del campamento.

En salud y SMAPS, equipos apoyados por la OIM atendieron a 297 personas en el Polideportivo José María Vargas, 185 personas en el Estadio César Nieves y 97 personas en Cayumar, Falcón, mediante servicios de medicina general, pediatría, medicina interna, ginecología, primeros auxilios psicológicos y apoyo psicosocial. La OIM también movilizó una planta potabilizadora de agua, continuó brindando orientación y referenciación en protección, proporcionó 13 asesorías legales, identificó la presencia de niñas, niños y adolescentes no acompañados en Falcón, capacitó enumeradores en la herramienta IMS y completó, junto con OCHA, tres encuestas de Evaluación Rápida de Necesidades en el municipio Silva.

Respuesta planificada de la OIM

La OIM continuará priorizando alojamiento temporal seguro, coordinación y gestión de campamentos transitorios, WASH, salud y SMAPS, protección, datos y evaluaciones de necesidades. En los campamentos transitorios, continuará apoyando la planificación de sitios, registro, gestión de almacenes, instalación de carpas, iluminación, puntos de carga, conectividad, participación comunitaria, provisión de información, AAP, mitigación de riesgos de VBG y medidas de PEAS.

En WASH, salud, protección y gestión de información, la OIM buscará ampliar el acceso a agua segura, saneamiento básico, promoción de higiene, servicios móviles de salud, referencias, atención de enfermedades crónicas, salud sexual y reproductiva, primeros auxilios psicológicos y SMAPS. Este documento también visibiliza necesidades de apoyo adicional para sostener y ampliar la asistencia humanitaria, mejorar las condiciones en campamentos transitorios y comunidades afectadas, fortalecer servicios seguros y con rendición de cuentas, reducir riesgos de protección y salud pública, y generar datos oportunos para orientar una respuesta más focalizada a las poblaciones afectadas.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Venezuela: Terremotos de junio de 2026 – Snapshot de situación (Última actualización: 2 de Julio de 2026) [ES/EN]
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Resumen de la situación

Doble sismo (M7,2 y M7,5) el 24 de junio, 18:00 h, en el eje San Felipe–Yumare–Montalbán (Yaracuy–Carabobo).

Réplicas en descenso: 86 el 28 de junio y 30 el 29 de junio, con menor magnitud promedio.

GDACS emitió Alerta Roja: 2,4 millones de personas expuestas a sacudidas severas.

La Guaira fue declarado Zona de Desastre, con afectaciones concentradas en Caraballeda y Catia La Mar.

Afectación severa también en Distrito Capital, Miranda, Carabobo, Yaracuy, Aragua y Falcón.

---

Situation overview

Double earthquake (M7.2 and M7.5) on June 24, 18:00, along the San Felipe–Yumare–Montalbán axis (Yaracuy–Carabobo).

Aftershocks declining:86 on June 28 and 30 on June 29, with lower average magnitude.

GDACS issued a Red Alert:2.4 million people exposed to severe shaking.

La Guaira was declared a Disaster Zone, with impacts concentrated in Caraballeda and Catia La Mar.

Severe impact also in Distrito Capital, Miranda, Carabobo, Yaracuy, Aragua and Falcón.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Lebanon: Flash Update #40 - Escalation of hostilities in Lebanon (as of 2 July 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • At least 4,298 deaths and 12,196 injuries due to hostilities recorded by the Ministry of Public Health since 2 March.
  • 646,107 people having returned to their areas of origin, while nearly 500,000 people remain displaced, including 47,143 Internally Displaced Persons (over 53 per cent women and girls) in 430 collective shelters as of 2 July.
  • Humanitarian and protection needs remain significant despite reduced hostilities. Daily reports of airstrikes, shelling, demolitions, drone activity, and UXO incidents continue to pose risks to civilians and hinder safe, voluntary, and dignified returns.
  • Access constraints and funding shortfalls continue to limit the response. Damage to infrastructure and service networks, together with insecurity in parts of South and Nabatieh governorates, continue to affect humanitarian operations, while the 2026 Lebanon Flash Appeal remains only 37 per cent funded.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.
HIGHLIGHTS
At least 4,298 deaths and 12,196 injuries due to hostilities recorded by the Ministry of Public Health since 2 March.
646,107 people having returned to their areas of origin, while nearly 500,000 people remain displaced, including 47,143 Internally Displaced Persons (over 53 per cent women and girls) in 430 collective shelters as of 2 July.
Humanitarian and protection needs remain significant despite reduced hostilities. Daily reports of airstrikes, shelling, demolitions, drone activity, and UXO incidents continue to pose risks to civilians and hinder safe, voluntary, and dignified returns.
Access constraints and funding shortfalls continue to limit the response. Damage to infrastructure and service networks, together with insecurity in parts of South and Nabatieh governorates, continue to affect humanitarian operations, while the 2026 Lebanon Flash Appeal remains only 37 per cent funded.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Haiti: Groupe de Travail sur les Transferts Monétaires en Haïti (GTTM/CBTWG) - Compte rendu de la réunion mensuelle du CBTWG (Date : 30 juin 2026) [Meeting Minutes]
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Haiti
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Le Groupe de Travail sur les Transferts Monétaires en Haïti (GTTM/CWG) est un groupe de coordination
humanitaire chargé d’organiser, harmoniser et améliorer l’assistance monétaire fournie par les différents acteurs
humanitaires. Cette plateforme d’échanges techniques regroupe en Haïti des institutions gouvernementales, des
prestataires de services financiers, des agences des Nations unies, et des organisations non gouvernementales
nationales et internationales qui interviennent dans les opérations de transfert monétaire / cash transfert. Ce
groupe vise notamment à améliorer la qualité des transferts monétaires à travers le développement d’une
approche stratégique de l’assistance monétaire afin qu’elle soit mieux intégrée dans la programmation humanitaire
et qu’elle soit adapter et plus bénéfique

items.title: Haiti: Groupe de Travail sur les Transferts Monétaires en Haïti (GTTM/CBTWG) - Compte rendu de la réunion mensuelle du CBTWG (Date : 24 juin 2026) [Meeting Minutes]
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Haiti
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Le Groupe de Travail sur les Transferts Monétaires en Haïti (GTTM/CWG) est un groupe de coordination
humanitaire chargé d’organiser, harmoniser et améliorer l’assistance monétaire fournie par les différents acteurs
humanitaires. Cette plateforme d’échanges techniques regroupe en Haïti des institutions gouvernementales, des
prestataires de services financiers, des agences des Nations unies, et des organisations non gouvernementales
nationales et internationales qui interviennent dans les opérations de transfert monétaire / cash transfert. Ce
groupe vise notamment à améliorer la qualité des transferts monétaires à travers le développement d’une
approche stratégique de l’assistance monétaire afin qu’elle soit mieux intégrée dans la programmation humanitaire
et qu’elle soit adapter et plus bénéfique

Country: Haiti
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Terremotos en Venezuela junio de 2026: Equipos de Búsqueda y Rescate Urbano (USAR) - Datos clave (Al 3 de julio, 10:00 GMT-4)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

A solicitud del Gobierno de Venezuela, equipos USAR de 29 países, con más de 2.400 integrantes y 188 perros de búsqueda, han llegado al país para localizar y rescatar a personas atrapadas bajo estructuras colapsadas y brindar atención médica inicial. Algunos equipos han comenzado a preparar su salida del país. La Oficina de Coordinación de Asuntos Humanitarios de las Naciones Unidas (OCHA) continúa brindando apoyo a los equipos.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Algunos equipos han comenzado a preparar su salida del país. La Oficina de Coordinación de Asuntos Humanitarios de las Naciones Unidas (OCHA) continúa brindando apoyo a los equipos.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Venezuela Earthquakes June 2026: Urban Search and Rescue (USAR) Team Snapshot (As of 3 July, 10:00 GMT-4)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

At the request of the Government of Venezuela, USAR teams from 29 countries, with more than 2,400 personnel and 188 search dogs, arrived in the country to locate and extract people trapped by collapsed structures and provide initial medical support. Some teams are now preparing to leave the country. The United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) continues to provide support to the teams.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: CAR: TABLEAU DE BORD HUMANITAIRE : RESPONSABILITÉ ENVERS LES PERSONNES AFFECTÉES (AAP) (janvier - mars 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Central African Republic
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Au premier trimestre 2026, l’AAP présente des progrès significatifs malgré la baisse des financements humanitaires. Au total, 22 987 représentants locaux ont été consultés dans 32 sous-préfectures, tandis que 129 572 personnes ont été sensibilisées, confirmant une intégration croissante de l’approche centrée sur les personnes affectées dans la planification et la réponse humanitaire. Les activités de renforcement des capacités ont permis de former 1 719 acteurs, dont 166 travailleurs humanitaires, reflétant une priorisation stratégique de l’AAP aux niveaux institutionnel et communautaire. Par ailleurs, l’utilisation des données communautaires dans les processus décisionnels s'est consolidée, avec 67 présentations AAP réalisées dans les cadres de coordination, contribuant à une meilleure prise en compte des retours communautaires dans l'orientation de la réponse humanitaire.
Cependant, la fermeture de la boucle de redevabilité demeure limitée : sur 17 746 plaintes et retours d'information collectés, seules 6 525 réponses ont été fournies, affectant l’efficacité des mécanismes de retour d'information et la confiance des communautés envers les acteurs humanitaires. Comparée à la même période en 2025, l’année 2026 montre ainsi une progression globale de l’AAP en matière d’engagement communautaire, de renforcement capacités et d’utilisation des données AAP. Ces avancées demeurent néanmoins fragiles dans un contexte de ressources limitées, nécessitant des efforts ciblés pour renforcer la capacité de réponse, consolider les mécanismes AAP et systématiser le retour ainsi que l’utilisation des données communautaires dans la réponse humanitaire.

Country: Central African Republic
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Par ailleurs, l’utilisation des données communautaires dans les processus décisionnels s'est consolidée, avec 67 présentations AAP réalisées dans les cadres de coordination, contribuant à une meilleure prise en compte des retours communautaires dans l'orientation de la réponse humanitaire.

items.creator: Action for Humanity
items.title: oPt: Under-capacity and out of time: 1,000 Days of limited aid access and humanitarian disaster in Gaza
items.author: Action for Humanity
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Action for Humanity

Please refer to the attached file.

Today we mark 1,000 days of crisis in Gaza. Humanitarian NGO Action For Humanity has analysed publicly available records relating to aid accessing Gaza since October 7th, finding that there have been more days with no aid entering Gaza, than days when the UN minimum target for aid trucks (150 per day) was met. The full report is attached here.

Further findings from the report:

  • On just 12% of days, the "UN minimum target" was met
  • On the average day, 76 trucks - just over 50% of the UN minimum aid truck target was met
  • There were at least 142 days in which no aid at all was allowed into the enclave

According to Action For Humanity CEO Othman Moqbel:

"For the past 1000 days, the humanitarian system in Gaza has been forced to operate amid the destruction of the very infrastructure it depends on. Hospitals have been bombed and rendered inoperable, schools have become displacement shelters instead of places of learning, and roads that once connected communities have become de-facto frontlines. In a crisis that has killed over 70,000 people, the basic conditions for civilian life have been steadily dismantled, and it's clear that even the minimum amount of aid needed in these communities is not able to get in to Gaza. We demand an immediate end to the restrictions placed on humanitarian access in Gaza, and are calling for an increase in support from donors and supporters."

From Action For Humanity programmes team members in Gaza:

"The word ‘home' has vanished from the vocabulary of most families in the Gaza Strip“

“when we talk about a thousand days of war, we are talking about a crisis of thirst and contamination and all of this is merciless.”

“Instead of our children holding pens, books, and notebooks, they are now holding water and jerrycans and running after aid trucks”

“The war hasn't just destroyed buildings; the war is destroying the future of an entire generation.”

Country: occupied Palestinian territory

On just 12% of days, the "UN minimum target" was met
On the average day, 76 trucks - just over 50% of the UN minimum aid truck target was met
There were at least 142 days in which no aid at all was allowed into the enclave
According to Action For Humanity CEO Othman Moqbel:
"For the past 1000 days, the humanitarian system in Gaza has been forced to operate amid the destruction of the very infrastructure it depends on. Hospitals have been bombed and rendered inoperable, schools have become displacement shelters instead of places of learning, and roads that once connected communities have become de-facto frontlines. In a crisis that has killed over 70,000 people, the basic conditions for civilian life have been steadily dismantled, and it's clear that even the minimum amount of aid needed in these communities is not able to get in to Gaza. We demand an immediate end to the restrictions placed on humanitarian access in Gaza, and are calling for an increase in support from donors and supporters."
From Action For Humanity programmes team members in Gaza:

“when we talk about a thousand days of war, we are talking about a crisis of thirst and contamination and all of this is merciless.”
“Instead of our children holding pens, books, and notebooks, they are now holding water and jerrycans and running after aid trucks”
“The war hasn't just destroyed buildings; the war is destroying the future of an entire generation.”

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: Attacks on Health Care in Myanmar: 10 - 23 June 2026
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

Data collected by Insecurity Insight shows that since the military coup on 01 February 2021 and 04 June 2026 at least 1,943 incidents of violence against or obstruction of access to health care took place across Myanmar:

  • equivalent to one attack on health care every day for more than five years
  • 65% of these attacks were attributed to the Myanmar Armed Forces (MAF)
  • 175 health workers were killed and 932 arrested
  • health facilities were damaged on at least 509 occasions

So far this year, at least seven health workers were killed by explosive weapons. Five health workers were killed in three incidents involving MAF airstrikes: two occurred at prisoner-of-war detention facilities under the control of opposition forces in Rakhine and Yangon, while the third involved the bombing of a hospital located in areas under the control of the PDF in Sagaing. A further two health workers were killed in two separate roadside landmine explosions reportedly attributed to opposition forces in Bago and Magway regions.

View the incidents on our interactive map. Download the data. Updated every Monday and includes information on weapons use, perpetrators and effects. For data enquiries, please get in touch. Data is continuously updated and numbers may change if/ when further information is made available. See here for methodology.

Country: Myanmar
Source: Insecurity Insight

65% of these attacks were attributed to the Myanmar Armed Forces (MAF)
175 health workers were killed and 932 arrested
health facilities were damaged on at least 509 occasions
So far this year, at least seven health workers were killed by explosive weapons. Five health workers were killed in three incidents involving MAF airstrikes: two occurred at prisoner-of-war detention facilities under the control of opposition forces in Rakhine and Yangon, while the third involved the bombing of a hospital located in areas under the control of the PDF in Sagaing. A further two health workers were killed in two separate roadside landmine explosions reportedly attributed to opposition forces in Bago and Magway regions.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: CAR: République centrafricaine: Plan préléminaire de préparation à la prévention et à la réponse à l'exploitation et aux abus sexuels dans le cadre de la réponse à la maladie à virus ébola MVE - (juillet 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Countries: Central African Republic, Democratic Republic of the Congo
Sources: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, UN Resident Coordinator in the Central African Republic

Please refer to the attached file.

La présence de cas de maladie à virus Ebola (MVE) en République démocratique du Congo (RDC) représente un risque sanitaire important pour les pays voisins, dont la République centrafricaine (RCA), en raison de la proximité géographique, des mouvements transfrontaliers de population, des échanges communautaires et commerciaux, ainsi que des vulnérabilités existantes des systèmes de surveillance, de prévention et de réponse. Dans ce contexte, la RCA doit renforcer sa préparation multisectorielle afin d’anticiper une éventuelle propagation de la MVE et de garantir une réponse rapide, coordonnée, sûre, inclusive et redevable envers les populations.

Les urgences sanitaires, notamment celles qui nécessitent le déploiement rapide d’équipes de riposte, de personnel de santé, de volontaires, de prestataires, de partenaires d’exécution et d’acteurs communautaires, peuvent accroître les risques d’exploitation et d’abus sexuels (EAS), de harcèlement sexuel (HS), d’abus de pouvoir, de mauvaise conduite, de discrimination et de représailles. Ces risques sont particulièrement élevés lorsque l’accès aux soins, à l’information, à l’assistance, aux services de protection ou aux mécanismes de plainte dépend de relations de pouvoir inégales entre les acteurs de la réponse et les populations affectées.

Dans le cadre de la préparation et de la réponse à la MVE, l’intégration de la protection contre l’exploitation et les abus sexuels (PEAS) constitue donc une exigence transversale essentielle. Elle vise à prévenir les risques d’EAS/HS, à renforcer la confiance des communautés dans les interventions sanitaires et humanitaires, à garantir l’accès à des mécanismes de plainte sûrs, confidentiels et accessibles, et à assurer une assistance rapide, éthique et centrée sur les survivant·e·s. Elle contribue également à améliorer la qualité, la crédibilité et la redevabilité de la réponse.

Le présent plan préliminaire propose des actions prioritaires et complémentaires à mettre en oeuvre par le point focal PEAS, la coordination inter-agences PEAS, les autorités nationales, les agences des Nations Unies, les ONG, les partenaires techniques et les acteurs communautaires impliqués dans les préparatifs de réponse à la MVE en RCA. Il couvre notamment la coordination et le plaidoyer, l’analyse des risques EAS/HS, la sensibilisation du personnel et des prestataires, les mécanismes de plainte et de référencement, l’engagement communautaire, les ressources humaines, la supervision, le suivi, la mobilisation des ressources et la documentation des apprentissages.
